q: A wire of length L and cross-sectional area A is stretched by a force F. If Young’s modulus is Y, the elongation produced is:
exp: Young’s modulus Y = (stress/strain) = (F/A)/(ΔL/L). So ΔL = FL/AY.

q: Two wires A and B of same material have lengths in ratio 1:2 and diameters in ratio 2:1. Ratio of their Young’s modulus is:
exp: Young’s modulus depends only on material, not dimensions.

q: A steel wire and a copper wire of same length and area are stretched by same force. Which has greater elongation?
exp: Copper has lower Young’s modulus, so larger extension.

q: Stress is defined as:
Strain is:
opts: Change in length per unit length

q: A wire breaks when stress exceeds 2×10^8 N/m². If area is doubled, breaking force becomes:
exp: F = stress × area, so doubling area doubles breaking force.

q: The slope of stress-strain curve in elastic region gives:
A wire of length L is stretched by x. If length becomes 2L (same material, same force), extension becomes:
exp: ΔL ∝ L, so doubling length doubles extension.

A rubber wire shows large strain. This indicates:
exp: Large strain for small stress means low Young’s modulus.

q: Bulk modulus relates to:
exp: Bulk modulus measures resistance to volume change.

q: A liquid is less compressible if it has:
exp: Higher bulk modulus means less compressibility.

q: Which deformation occurs in shear stress?
exp: Shear stress causes shape change without volume change.
